summary: Three psalms of David span the spectrum from composite praise (Psalm 108, drawn from Psalms 57 and 60) to fierce imprecation against a treacherous enemy (Psalm 109, later applied to Judas in Acts 1) to the Old Testament's most frequently quoted messianic oracle (Psalm 110), which declares the coming Lord to be both king at God's right hand and eternal priest after the order of Melchizedek.
psalm_rows_html:
PsalmTypeAuthorSituationKey ThemeNotable VerseNT Connection
108Composite Praise & ConfidenceDavidCompiled from Psalm 57:7–11 and Psalm 60:5–12; a prayer for military deliverance and confident trust in God's sovereignty over the nationsGod's sovereign dominion over all nations; human help is vain but with God victory is certain"With God we shall do valiantly; it is he who will tread down our foes." (v. 13)The universal praise of v. 3 ("I will give thanks among the peoples") anticipates the Gentile mission; cf. Romans 15:9
109Imprecatory LamentDavidSurrounded by slanderous enemies who repaid David's love with accusations and hatred; the most sustained imprecatory psalm in the PsalterGod as defender of the poor and needy; the punishment of those who love to curse and despise mercy"May his days be few; may another take his office!" (v. 8)Peter quotes v. 8 in Acts 1:20 as fulfilled in the betrayal of Judas and the subsequent selection of Matthias as his replacement
110Royal / Messianic OracleDavidA divine oracle addressed to the Davidic king, declaring his exaltation to God's right hand and his eternal appointment as priest-king after the order of MelchizedekThe Messiah's dual office of eternal king and eternal priest; his ultimate victory over all enemies"The LORD says to my Lord: 'Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool.'" (v. 1)The most frequently quoted OT psalm in the NT: Jesus uses v. 1 to stump the Pharisees (Matt 22:41–46); Peter applies it to the resurrection and ascension (Acts 2:34–35); Hebrews 5–7 expounds the Melchizedekian priesthood of Christ from v. 4
